title: SEGMENT 12 — Metatranscriptomics, metaproteomics, metabolomics: the multi-omics view

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — Metatranscriptomics, metaproteomics, metabolomics: the multi-omics view
DNA tells you what's possible.

To know what's actually happening in a microbial community at a given moment, you need to measure the next layers of biological information: which genes are being expressed, which proteins are being made, and which metabolites are being produced and consumed.
These are the domains of metatranscriptomics, metaproteomics, and metabolomics — the multi-omics view of the microbiome.
Let me build the picture layer by layer.
Metagenomics, which we've been discussing, gives you the DNA — the gene content of the community.
It tells you what the community could potentially do, but not what it's actually doing.
A bacterium might carry a gene for a pathway that it only expresses under specific conditions; from DNA alone, you can't tell if that pathway is on or off in this particular sample.
Metatranscriptomics is the same idea applied to RNA.

You extract total RNA from the community, you sequence it (usually after depleting the highly abundant ribosomal RNA), and the result is a snapshot of which genes are being actively transcribed at the moment of sampling.
Highly expressed genes are represented by many reads; silent genes are absent.
The output is a community-level gene expression profile, telling you not just what the community could do but what it's doing right now.

The technical challenges are real — RNA is much less stable than DNA, so sample collection and preservation have to be fast and careful; the dominance of rRNA means most of your reads are uninformative unless you deplete it; and the dynamic range of expression is huge, so you need deep sequencing to detect lowly expressed transcripts.
But when it works, metatranscriptomics is the difference between knowing what tools are in the workshop and knowing which ones are actually being used.
Metaproteomics goes one step further by measuring the proteins themselves — the actual workhorses of the cell — rather than the transcripts encoding them.

Proteins are measured by mass spectrometry: you digest community proteins into peptides, you run them through a mass spectrometer, and you identify peptides by matching their masses and fragmentation patterns against predicted proteins from a metagenome-derived database.
The output is a list of proteins detected and their relative abundances.

Metaproteomics is technically harder than metatranscriptomics because mass spectrometry is less sensitive than sequencing, and because protein identification depends on having a good reference database — usually metagenome-derived from the same sample — to match peptide masses against.
The dynamic range of protein detection is also more limited than RNA.

But proteins are closer to function than transcripts are; not every transcript is translated, not every translated protein is stable, so the protein-level measurement is the most direct readout of what the community is actually doing.
Metabolomics measures the small molecules — the metabolites — being produced and consumed in the community.

These are the actual chemical outputs of microbial metabolism: short-chain fatty acids, bile acid derivatives, amino acid metabolites, vitamins, neuroactive compounds, the entire chemical conversation between microbes and between microbes and the host.
Metabolomics is also done by mass spectrometry, usually combined with chromatographic separation (HPLC-MS, GC-MS), and the output is a list of detected metabolites with their abundances.
Metabolomics is the layer most directly relevant to host physiology — the metabolites produced by your gut microbiome are what actually circulate in your blood and signal to your tissues.
If you're trying to understand mechanistically how a microbiome difference affects host health, metabolomics is often the most informative layer.
The combination of all these — DNA, RNA, protein, metabolites — is called multi-omics, and it is the gold standard for microbiome studies that aim to do mechanism rather than just description.

The HMP phase-two integrative project specifically applied multi-omics across their disease cohorts; modern high-end microbiome studies often integrate at least metagenomics, metabolomics, and host transcriptomics or proteomics.
The analogy.

If a microbial community is a factory, metagenomics is the inventory of machines on the floor, metatranscriptomics is the daily production schedule, metaproteomics is the actual count of workers operating each machine, and metabolomics is the products coming off the assembly line.
To understand what the factory really makes, you want all four layers.
To understand why it sometimes makes the wrong things — disease-associated states — you also need to integrate with measurements of the customer, the host.
The full multi-omics view is expensive, technically demanding, and analytically complex, but it's the closest we can currently get to a mechanistic picture of microbiome function.
There's also the integration problem.
Each of these omics layers produces its own data type with its own peculiarities, and combining them statistically is nontrivial.
There's a whole subfield of multi-omics integration developing methods — joint dimensionality reduction, network-based analysis, machine learning approaches — for finding signal across layers.
The field is young and the tools are still rough, but it's where microbiome science is heading.
Single-layer studies are increasingly seen as preliminary; the rigorous mechanistic work integrates many layers.
The cost story matters here too.
Metagenomics has become cheap.
Metatranscriptomics is moderately expensive.
Metaproteomics is expensive and slow.
Metabolomics is moderately expensive but requires expertise and instrumentation that not every lab has.
So even though everyone agrees multi-omics is the right approach, in practice most studies still use just one or two layers, and most public datasets are metagenomics-only.
The full multi-omics treatment is what you do for your flagship study, not your screening study.
